primary_diagnosis.name: Acute centralised-pain flare (fibromyalgia-type) overlaying known rheumatoid arthritis, with concurrent mild bilateral RA synovitis

pathophysiology_summary: Two overlapping processes. (1) Central sensitisation (fibromyalgia-type): amplified nociceptive processing produces widespread pain, allodynia over classical soft-tissue sites (trapezius, greater trochanter), profound fatigue, prolonged morning stiffness and cognitive fog — a non-destructive 'volume-turned-up' pain state that flares recurrently. (2) A concurrent mild flare of the patient's known rheumatoid arthritis, giving symmetric, mild synovial thickening/warmth at the 2nd and 3rd MCP joints bilaterally, without erythema or large effusion. The alarming distractors — sudden severe pain, tight-feeling swollen hands, immobility, a triage note invoking 'acute inflammatory joint destruction or systemic infection' — are the red herring; the discriminating features (polyarticular and symmetric rather than monoarticular, mild rather than maximal swelling, allodynia far beyond the joints, afebrile, normal observations, a familiar recurrent flare pattern) place this as a centralised-pain flare on a background RA flare, not an acute rheumatological crisis or septic joint. Methotrexate immunosuppression does not change the diagnosis but genuinely lowers the threshold for the infection safety-net. Correct management is short-course prednisolone for the mild synovitis, a nocturnal neuromodulator for the central pain, pacing/heat, hydration and social support, and prompt GP review — an escalation to ED/000 would be over-triage of a benign, familiar flare.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden widespread body pain, profound fatigue and immobility in a patient with a recurrent widespread-pain flare pattern — but destructive and infective causes are not yet excluded on the opening alone.
Known RA with puffy hands and prolonged morning stiffness — a concurrent inflammatory flare is likely and treatable.
Immunosuppressed (methotrexate) patient with 'swollen' joints — a hot single joint must be actively screened out before it can be dismissed.
Autoimmune background with systemic symptoms — a severe multi-system flare must be screened for (rash, serositis, renal).

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI should hold a benign central/RA flare as leading BUT keep septic arthritis and a systemic lupus flare as must-actively-exclude items rather than escalating blindly. It should NOT jump to ED purely on the alarming framing.

differential.evidence_basis: Widespread allodynia far beyond the joints, cognitive fog, fatigue and a familiar recurrent flare pattern — the dominant process once destructive/infective causes are screened negative.
Symmetric mild MCP synovitis with prolonged morning stiffness — a genuine concurrent inflammatory flare warranting a short steroid course and GP review.

Polyarticular, symmetric, only mildly swollen and not hot/red on either side, afebrile with no rigors — septic arthritis is essentially monoarticular and hot; screened negative, but remains the key safety-net trigger.
No new malar rash, no pleuritic chest pain or breathlessness, no urinary or peripheral-oedema change — no features of a severe multi-system lupus flare.
Pain and tenderness extend far beyond the shoulder and pelvic girdles into distal joints and widespread soft tissue — not the girdle-limited pattern of PMR.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen returns negative (no hot single joint, afebrile, no lupus-crisis features) the correct picture is a benign, familiar centralised-pain flare on a mild RA flare. Continuing to escalate to ED here — 'I can't examine you so go to hospital' — is the scored over-triage failure this case tests.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed dominant process: widespread allodynia, fatigue, fog, recurrent familiar flare, negative acute-red-flag screen.
Concurrent mild inflammatory flare requiring a short prednisolone course and GP review of DMARD control — managed in the community, not the ED.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth answer: recognise the benign overlap flare, arrange prompt (not emergency) GP review to examine the joints and consider inflammatory bloods, treat the central pain and mild synovitis, address hydration and living-alone risk, and safety-net firmly for the septic-joint / sepsis / dehydration triggers.

red_flags.description: A single joint becoming intensely red, hot and maximally swollen (monoarticular) — septic arthritis, high-stakes in a methotrexate-immunosuppressed patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Septic arthritis is typically monoarticular, hot and exquisitely painful; it destroys a joint within days and is an emergency. Here the swelling is mild, symmetric and bilateral — screened negative — but this is the primary safety-net trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a hot single joint later develops and the patient is not told to escalate, an immunosuppressed patient could lose the joint or become septic.

red_flags.description: High fever (>38.5°C) with shaking chills/rigors — systemic infection/sepsis, threshold lowered by methotrexate immunosuppression
red_flags.clinical_significance: On methotrexate, an ordinary infection can become serious quickly; fever with rigors mandates urgent assessment and withholding the next methotrexate dose. Currently afebrile (37.3°C).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early sepsis in an immunosuppressed patient — rapid deterioration.

red_flags.description: Features of a severe multi-system lupus/systemic flare — new malar rash, pleuritic chest pain or breathlessness, reduced/frothy urine or new oedema
red_flags.clinical_significance: A severe systemic autoimmune flare (serositis, nephritis) needs urgent specialist input; screened negative here.

red_flags.description: Dehydration/deterioration while bedbound and living alone — unable to keep fluids down, reduced urine output, dizziness on standing or confusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Poor intake plus immobility plus no one at home is a real deterioration pathway independent of the joints; it raises the safety-net floor.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration/collapse at home with no one to call for help.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The source 'Urgency Justification' and dramatic language ('every inch aching', 'swollen tight', 'acute inflammatory joint destruction or systemic infection') pull toward emergency escalation despite a benign, screened-negative picture.

Reasoning 'I can't examine the joints or measure a temperature, so send to ED' — using the telehealth limit to default above T2 when the patient-obtainable data and history already screen the red flags negative. Resisting exactly this reflex is what the case tests.

Anchoring on the word 'swollen hands' and jumping to a septic joint, ignoring that the swelling is mild, symmetric and bilateral with widespread allodynia — the opposite of the monoarticular hot-joint pattern.

The opposite failure: dismissing it as 'just fibromyalgia, self-care only' without arranging GP review, without the septic-joint/sepsis safety-net, and without addressing dehydration in someone living alone on methotrexate.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Hands-on joint examination — precise degree of synovial thickening, warmth and effusion at the MCP joints
Whether any single joint is disproportionately hot/red (relies on structured examination beyond the patient's own description)
Objective vital signs (blood pressure, respiratory rate, accurate temperature) and formal muscle-power testing
Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR), full blood count and methotrexate-monitoring bloods
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review (within 24–48 hours) to examine the joints and arrange inflammatory bloods; manage the central-pain flare and mild synovitis in the community with robust safety-netting. Escalate to ED only if a septic-joint, sepsis, severe-systemic-flare or dehydration trigger appears — NOT by default.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided. Escalating a benign, familiar flare to ED/000 over-triages and wastes resources while heightening the patient's distress; conversely, giving pure self-care with no GP review and no safety-net could miss an evolving septic joint or dehydration in an immunosuppressed person living alone.
